County Tariff Exposure: Guided Student Workbook

Ian Helfrich

2026-03-04

  • How To Use This Workbook
  • 0) Preflight: Make Sure We Are In The Project Root
  • 1) Start With Respect: What She Got Right
    • Checkpoint
  • 2) Diagnose The Original Pipeline (Without Throwing It Away)
    • 2.1 Look At The Legacy Script Inputs
    • 2.2 Why This Matters
    • Checkpoint
  • 3) Load The Upgraded County Panel
    • 3.1 One Real Data Cleaning Note: State Name Gaps
  • 4) Reconstruct The Exposure Formula From Components
    • 4.1 Example: Hoonah-Angoon Census Area
    • Checkpoint
  • 5) NAICS Problem Scanner (Teaching + Debugging)
  • 6) Exposure Levels vs Exposure Intensity
    • Checkpoint
  • 7) Visual Diagnostics In R (Static Maps)
  • 8) Regression Ladder (HC1, FE, Clustered)
    • Checkpoint
  • 9) Interpretation Drill (Write Like A Researcher)
  • 10) Optional Extension: Campaign Finance Layer
  • 11) Teaching Exit Ticket
  • 12) Rebuild Commands (If You Need To Refresh Everything)

How To Use This Workbook

This notebook is built for a discussion-first tutoring flow.

The goal is not to sprint to coefficients. The goal is to understand what each object means, what each design choice buys us, and what each estimate can honestly claim.

Suggested flow in session: read each section heading and short note out loud, run the chunk, then pause for the checkpoint question before moving on.

0) Preflight: Make Sure We Are In The Project Root

if (file.exists("retaliatory-tariff.Rproj")) {
  project_root <- "."
} else if (file.exists("../retaliatory-tariff.Rproj")) {
  project_root <- ".."
} else {
  stop("Could not find retaliatory-tariff.Rproj. Open this notebook inside the project folder.")
}

setwd(project_root)
knitr::opts_knit$set(root.dir = normalizePath(getwd()))
cat("Working directory set to:", normalizePath(getwd()), "\n")
Working directory set to: /Users/ian/gemini_playground/tutoring/Jubili_RCoding/retaliatory-tariff 
core_files <- c(
  "data/data_cleaning_code/build_county_exposure.R",
  "data/data_cleaning_code/clean_tariff_retaliation.R",
  "code/overhaul_open_data_panel.R",
  "data/public/derived/county_tariff_exposure_panel_with_votes_2017_2023.csv",
  "data/public/derived/county_industry_tariff_panel_2017_2023.csv"
)

missing_core <- core_files[!file.exists(core_files)]
if (length(missing_core) > 0) {
  stop("Missing required files:\n", paste("-", missing_core, collapse = "\n"))
}

cat("Preflight check passed. Core files are present.\n")
Preflight check passed. Core files are present.
needed <- c(
  "readr", "dplyr", "tidyr", "stringr", "ggplot2", "scales",
  "knitr", "fixest", "modelsummary", "sf", "tigris"
)

missing_pkgs <- needed[!vapply(needed, requireNamespace, quietly = TRUE, FUN.VALUE = logical(1))]
if (length(missing_pkgs) > 0) {
  stop(
    "Install missing packages first:\n",
    paste("install.packages('", missing_pkgs, "')", collapse = "\n")
  )
}

suppressPackageStartupMessages({
  library(readr)
  library(dplyr)
  library(tidyr)
  library(stringr)
  library(ggplot2)
  library(scales)
  library(knitr)
})

1) Start With Respect: What She Got Right

Her first instinct was excellent.

The skeleton of the measure was already in the Bartik/shift-share family:

\[ \text{Local Exposure} \approx \text{Shock Magnitude} \times \text{Local Reliance} \]

The upgrade is not about replacing her idea. It is about improving the geography of the shock and the scaling of intensity.

Checkpoint

In your own words, what is the difference between replacing an idea and refining an idea?

2) Diagnose The Original Pipeline (Without Throwing It Away)

2.1 Look At The Legacy Script Inputs

legacy_exports <- read_csv("data/tools/export_with_fips.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)

legacy_fips <- legacy_exports %>%
  mutate(fips_code = str_replace_all(fips_code, ";", ",")) %>%
  separate_rows(fips_code, sep = ",") %>%
  mutate(
    fips_code = str_trim(fips_code),
    county_fips = str_extract(fips_code, "[0-9]{5}")
  )

legacy_summary <- legacy_fips %>%
  summarise(
    raw_rows = n(),
    unique_districts = n_distinct(District),
    unique_counties = n_distinct(county_fips, na.rm = TRUE),
    share_missing_fips = mean(is.na(county_fips))
  )

kable(legacy_summary, digits = 3)
raw_rows unique_districts unique_counties share_missing_fips
100259 46 53 0.035

2.2 Why This Matters

If local assignment is based mainly on customs district geography, we risk confusing where goods exit with where goods are produced.

That is the core “Laredo problem” in one line.

Checkpoint

Why can a port-heavy county look artificially exposed in this setup?

3) Load The Upgraded County Panel

panel <- read_csv(
  "data/public/derived/county_tariff_exposure_panel_with_votes_2017_2023.csv",
  show_col_types = FALSE
) %>%
  mutate(
    county_fips = str_pad(as.character(county_fips), 5, pad = "0"),
    year = as.integer(year)
  )

panel %>%
  summarise(
    rows = n(),
    years = n_distinct(year),
    counties = n_distinct(county_fips),
    min_year = min(year, na.rm = TRUE),
    max_year = max(year, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) %>%
  kable()
rows years counties min_year max_year
22659 7 3253 2017 2023 
panel %>%
  group_by(year) %>%
  summarise(counties = n_distinct(county_fips), .groups = "drop") %>%
  kable()
year counties
2017 3235
2018 3235
2019 3237
2020 3238
2021 3238
2022 3239
2023 3237

3.1 One Real Data Cleaning Note: State Name Gaps

state_gap <- panel %>%
  mutate(statefp = str_sub(county_fips, 1, 2)) %>%
  summarise(
    missing_state_name = sum(is.na(state_name) | state_name == ""),
    share_missing = mean(is.na(state_name) | state_name == "")
  )

kable(state_gap, digits = 3)
missing_state_name share_missing
943 0.042 
panel %>%
  mutate(statefp = str_sub(county_fips, 1, 2)) %>%
  filter(is.na(state_name) | state_name == "") %>%
  count(statefp, sort = TRUE) %>%
  kable()
statefp n
72 546
02 184
66 117
09 40
69 21
78 21
60 14

This is worth teaching directly: a strong design still needs messy-data honesty.

4) Reconstruct The Exposure Formula From Components

Now we open the county-industry panel and verify the math at the row level.

components <- read_csv(
  "data/public/derived/county_industry_tariff_panel_2017_2023.csv",
  show_col_types = FALSE
) %>%
  mutate(
    county_fips = str_pad(as.character(county_fips), 5, pad = "0"),
    year = as.integer(year)
  )

components %>%
  select(county_fips, county_name, year, naics2c, emp_share, export_value_usd,
         tariff_rate_mean, county_tariff_component_mean) %>%
  slice_head(n = 5) %>%
  kable(digits = 4)
county_fips county_name year naics2c emp_share export_value_usd tariff_rate_mean county_tariff_component_mean
01001 Autauga County, Alabama 2017 11 0.0084 79230432234 0.2589 11627099
01003 Baldwin County, Alabama 2017 11 0.0002 79230432234 0.2589 1250226
01005 Barbour County, Alabama 2017 11 0.0218 79230432234 0.2589 18628363
01007 Bibb County, Alabama 2017 11 0.0212 79230432234 0.2589 9251670
01009 Blount County, Alabama 2017 11 0.0011 79230432234 0.2589 875158

4.1 Example: Hoonah-Angoon Census Area

focus_county <- components %>%
  filter(str_detect(county_name, "Hoonah-Angoon")) %>%
  distinct(county_fips, county_name) %>%
  slice(1)

focus_fips <- focus_county$county_fips[[1]]
focus_name <- focus_county$county_name[[1]]
focus_year <- max(panel$year, na.rm = TRUE)

cat("Focus county:", focus_name, "| FIPS:", focus_fips, "| Year:", focus_year, "\n")
Focus county: Hoonah-Angoon Census Area, Alaska | FIPS: 02105 | Year: 2023 
focus_components <- components %>%
  filter(county_fips == focus_fips, year == focus_year) %>%
  mutate(
    reconstructed_component = emp_share * export_value_usd * tariff_rate_mean
  ) %>%
  select(naics2c, emp_share, export_value_usd, tariff_rate_mean,
         county_tariff_component_mean, reconstructed_component) %>%
  arrange(desc(reconstructed_component))

focus_components %>%
  slice_head(n = 12) %>%
  kable(digits = 5)
naics2c emp_share export_value_usd tariff_rate_mean county_tariff_component_mean reconstructed_component
22 0.03361 0 0 0 0
23 0.05602 0 0 0 0
44-45 0.22409 0 0 0 0
48-49 0.15966 0 0 0 0
53 0.00840 0 0 0 0
62 0.15126 0 0 0 0
71 0.15966 0 0 0 0
72 0.08123 0 0 0 0 
reconstructed <- components %>%
  group_by(county_fips, year) %>%
  summarise(reconstructed_exposure = sum(county_tariff_component_mean, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

comparison <- panel %>%
  select(county_fips, year, county_tariff_exposure_mean) %>%
  inner_join(reconstructed, by = c("county_fips", "year")) %>%
  mutate(abs_diff = abs(county_tariff_exposure_mean - reconstructed_exposure))

comparison %>%
  summarise(
    max_abs_diff = max(abs_diff, na.rm = TRUE),
    median_abs_diff = median(abs_diff, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) %>%
  kable(digits = 6)
max_abs_diff median_abs_diff
1e-06 0

Checkpoint

Why is this reconstruction test one of the most important trust checks in the whole project?

5) NAICS Problem Scanner (Teaching + Debugging)

emp_share_panel <- read_csv(
  "data/public/derived/county_industry_emp_share_2017_2023.csv",
  show_col_types = FALSE
) %>%
  mutate(
    county_fips = str_pad(as.character(county_fips), 5, pad = "0"),
    naics2c = as.character(naics2c),
    valid_naics = str_detect(naics2c, "^(00|[0-9]{2}|31-33|44-45|48-49|99)$"),
    emp = as.numeric(emp),
    emp_share = as.numeric(emp_share)
  )

naics_issues <- emp_share_panel %>%
  mutate(
    issue_type = case_when(
      !valid_naics ~ "bad_naics_format",
      is.na(emp) ~ "missing_emp",
      is.na(emp_share) ~ "missing_emp_share",
      !is.finite(emp_share) ~ "nonfinite_emp_share",
      TRUE ~ "ok"
    )
  ) %>%
  filter(issue_type != "ok") %>%
  count(year, issue_type, naics2c, sort = TRUE, name = "rows")

naics_issues %>% slice_head(n = 25) %>% kable()
year issue_type naics2c rows
2017 missing_emp_share 21 1
2017 missing_emp_share 44-45 1
2017 missing_emp_share 48-49 1
2017 missing_emp_share 56 1
2017 missing_emp_share 62 1
2017 missing_emp_share 72 1 
dir.create("output/analysis/tables", recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)
write_csv(naics_issues, "output/analysis/tables/naics_problem_entries_guided.csv")
cat("Wrote: output/analysis/tables/naics_problem_entries_guided.csv\n")
Wrote: output/analysis/tables/naics_problem_entries_guided.csv

6) Exposure Levels vs Exposure Intensity

This is the “Los Angeles problem” checkpoint: level and per-worker exposure should both be inspected.

latest_year <- max(panel$year, na.rm = TRUE)
latest <- panel %>% filter(year == latest_year)

level_top <- latest %>%
  arrange(desc(county_tariff_exposure_mean)) %>%
  select(county_name, county_fips, county_tariff_exposure_mean,
         county_tariff_exposure_per_worker, county_emp_total) %>%
  slice_head(n = 15)

intensity_top <- latest %>%
  arrange(desc(county_tariff_exposure_per_worker)) %>%
  select(county_name, county_fips, county_tariff_exposure_mean,
         county_tariff_exposure_per_worker, county_emp_total) %>%
  slice_head(n = 15)

cat("Top 15 by level\n")
Top 15 by level
kable(level_top, digits = 3)
county_name county_fips county_tariff_exposure_mean county_tariff_exposure_per_worker county_emp_total

county_name county_fips county_tariff_exposure_mean county_tariff_exposure_per_worker county_emp_total

Checkpoint

Which counties jump in rank once we normalize by employment, and why?

7) Visual Diagnostics In R (Static Maps)

latest_map <- latest %>%
  transmute(
    GEOID = county_fips,
    county_name,
    exposure = county_tariff_exposure_mean,
    exposure_per_worker = county_tariff_exposure_per_worker,
    swingness_20
  )
options(tigris_use_cache = TRUE)

counties_sf <- tigris::counties(cb = TRUE, year = 2023, class = "sf") %>%
  sf::st_as_sf() %>%
  select(GEOID, geometry)

map_df <- counties_sf %>%
  left_join(latest_map, by = "GEOID") %>%
  filter(!str_sub(GEOID, 1, 2) %in% c("02", "15", "60", "66", "69", "72", "78"))

p_map <- ggplot(map_df) +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = exposure_per_worker), color = NA) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "C", trans = "log1p", na.value = "grey92",
                       labels = label_number(accuracy = 0.1)) +
  labs(
    title = paste0("County tariff exposure per worker (", latest_year, ")"),
    subtitle = "Log-scaled fill to avoid outlier flattening",
    fill = "Exposure / worker"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(panel.grid = element_blank())

p_map

8) Regression Ladder (HC1, FE, Clustered)

suppressPackageStartupMessages({
  library(fixest)
  library(modelsummary)
})

reg_df <- panel %>%
  mutate(
    y = log1p(county_tariff_exposure_mean),
    state_name = as.character(state_name)
  ) %>%
  filter(
    is.finite(y),
    !is.na(gop_share_20),
    !is.na(swingness_20),
    !is.na(tariffed_emp_share)
  )

m1 <- feols(y ~ gop_share_20 + swingness_20 + tariffed_emp_share,
            data = reg_df,
            vcov = "hetero")

m2 <- feols(y ~ gop_share_20 + swingness_20 + tariffed_emp_share | state_name,
            data = reg_df %>% filter(!is.na(state_name), state_name != ""),
            vcov = "hetero")

m3 <- feols(y ~ gop_share_20 + swingness_20 + tariffed_emp_share | state_name,
            data = reg_df %>% filter(!is.na(state_name), state_name != ""),
            vcov = ~state_name)

modelsummary(
  list(
    "M1: HC1" = m1,
    "M2: +State FE (HC1)" = m2,
    "M3: +State FE (Clustered)" = m3
  ),
  stars = FALSE,
  fmt = 3,
  output = "markdown"
)
M1: HC1 M2: +State FE (HC1) M3: +State FE (Clustered)
(Intercept) 15.528
(0.253)
gop_share_20 -5.655 -5.152 -5.152
(0.255) (0.267) (1.171)
swingness_20 3.312 3.152 3.152
(0.177) (0.178) (0.711)
tariffed_emp_share 14.952 14.190 14.190
(0.277) (0.274) (1.737)
Num.Obs. 21717 21677 21677
R2 0.292 0.365 0.365
R2 Adj. 0.292 0.363 0.363
R2 Within 0.236 0.236
R2 Within Adj. 0.236 0.236
AIC 115733.4 113281.4 113281.4
BIC 115765.3 113696.6 113696.6
RMSE 3.47 3.29 3.29
Std.Errors Heteroskedasticity-robust Heteroskedasticity-robust by: state_name
FE: state_name X X

Checkpoint

Which coefficient is most stable across M1, M2, and M3? Which one is most sensitive?

9) Interpretation Drill (Write Like A Researcher)

Use this structure when she writes findings:

  1. What changed in the model? (specification and SE type)
  2. What stayed stable? (sign and rough magnitude)
  3. What became less certain? (wider intervals, clustering impact)
  4. What can we claim? (associational vs causal discipline)

Write 3-5 sentences now using the last table.

10) Optional Extension: Campaign Finance Layer

# Run this first if needed:
# source("code/add_campaign_finance_county_measures.R")

finance_panel <- read_csv(
  "data/public/derived/county_tariff_panel_with_campaign_finance.csv",
  show_col_types = FALSE
)

finance_panel %>%
  select(year, county_fips, donations_total, spending_total,
         donations_per_worker, spending_per_worker) %>%
  slice_head(n = 10)

11) Teaching Exit Ticket

Before ending the session, ask her to answer these out loud:

  1. What exactly is the shift in this project?
  2. What exactly is the share in this project?
  3. Why can customs-based geography mislead local exposure measurement?
  4. Why do we report both level and per-worker exposure?
  5. Why does clustered inference matter in county data?

If she can answer those clearly, the project has moved from coding exercise to research design.

12) Rebuild Commands (If You Need To Refresh Everything)

source("setup_project.R")
source("code/overhaul_open_data_panel.R")
source("code/validate_core_outputs.R")
source("code/build_analysis_outputs.R")
source("code/build_site_data.R")
source("code/build_bivariate_maps.R")
